examples.title: Algorithm: Basic MPI Program Structure
examples.description: Pseudocode demonstrating the fundamental structure of an MPI program including initialization, rank/size queries, and finalization
examples.code:
ALGORITHM BasicMPIProgram
INPUT: Command line arguments (argc, argv)
OUTPUT: Process rank and total size printed by each process

BEGIN
 // Step 1: Initialize MPI environment
 CALL MPI_Init(argc, argv)

 // Step 2: Get the rank (unique ID) of current process
 CALL MPI_Comm_rank(MPI_COMM_WORLD, rank)

 // Step 3: Get total number of processes
 CALL MPI_Comm_size(MPI_COMM_WORLD, size)

 // Step 4: Each process performs its computation
 PRINT "Hello from process " + rank + " of " + size

 // Step 5: Perform point-to-point communication if needed
 IF rank == 0 THEN
 data = 42
 CALL MPI_Send(data, count, MPI_INT, dest=1, tag=0, comm=MPI_COMM_WORLD)
 ELSE IF rank == 1 THEN
 CALL MPI_Recv(data, count, MPI_INT, source=0, tag=0, comm=MPI_COMM_WORLD, status)
 PRINT "Received: " + data
 END IF

 // Step 6: Finalize MPI environment
 CALL MPI_Finalize()
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows the essential structure of any MPI program: 1) Initialize with MPI_Init, 2) Query process identity with MPI_Comm_rank, 3) Query total processes with MPI_Comm_size, 4) Perform communication/computation, 5) Clean up with MPI_Finalize. The point-to-point send/receive demonstrates matching communication pairs.

examples.title: Python: Point-to-Point Communication with mpi4py
examples.description: Python implementation using mpi4py library demonstrating send and receive operations between processes
examples.code:
"""
MPI Point-to-Point Communication Example using mpi4py
This program demonstrates basic send/receive operations between processes.
"""

from mpi4py import MPI
import sys

def main():
 # Initialize MPI environment
 comm = MPI.COMM_WORLD
 
 # Get rank (process ID) and size (total processes)
 rank = comm.Get_rank()
 size = comm.Get_size()
 
 # Ensure we have at least 2 processes
 if size < 2:
 if rank == 0:
 print("This program requires at least 2 processes. Run with: mpirun -np 2 python program.py")
 sys.exit(1)
 
 # Prepare data for communication
 data = 100 if rank == 0 else 0
 
 # Point-to-point communication: Process 0 sends to Process 1
 if rank == 0:
 # Send data to process 1
 comm.send(data, dest=1, tag=0)
 print(f"Process {rank}: Sent data = {data} to process 1")
 
 elif rank == 1:
 # Receive data from process 0
 received_data = comm.recv(source=0, tag=0)
 print(f"Process {rank}: Received data = {received_data} from process 0")
 
 # Demonstrate ring communication - each process passes data to next
 # First, initialize token for ring
 if rank == 0:
 token = 0
 comm.send(token, dest=1, tag=1)
 print(f"Process {rank}: Started ring with token = {token}")
 received = comm.recv(source=size-1, tag=1)
 print(f"Process {rank}: Ring complete. Final token = {received}")
 else:
 token = comm.recv(source=rank-1, tag=1)
 new_token = token + rank
 comm.send(new_token, dest=(rank+1) % size, tag=1)

if __name__ == "__main__":
 main()
examples.explanation: This Python implementation uses mpi4py, the Python binding for MPI. It demonstrates: 1) Initializing MPI and getting rank/size, 2) Basic send/receive between two processes, 3) Ring communication pattern where each process passes data to the next. The comm.send() and comm.recv() methods provide a cleaner Pythonic interface while maintaining MPI semantics.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for n processes in ring communication
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per process for communication buffers

examples.title: C: Collective Communication with MPI_Bcast
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating broadcast operation where one process distributes data to all other processes
examples.explanation: This C program demonstrates collective communication with MPI_Bcast and MPI_Gather. MPI_Bcast distributes data from one root process to all other processes - after the broadcast, every process has the same data value. MPI_Gather collects data from all processes back to the root. The example shows a practical pattern: broadcast input data, compute locally, gather results. MPI_Barrier ensures all processes synchronize before the broadcast.
examples.timeComplexity: O(log n) for broadcast, O(n) for gather
examples.spaceComplexity: O(1) per process, O(n) at root for gather

examples.title: C: Parallel Sum with MPI_Reduce
examples.description: C implementation demonstrating parallel computation using reduction operation to sum values across all processes
examples.explanation: This comprehensive example demonstrates parallel computation using MPI_Reduce. Key concepts: 1) Work distribution - dividing array chunks among processes based on rank, 2) Local computation - each process computes its partial sum independently, 3) MPI_Reduce - combines all partial sums into a single result at the root using MPI_SUM operation, 4) MPI_Allreduce - similar to reduce but broadcasts result to all processes. The program also shows other reduction operations like MPI_MAX. This pattern is fundamental to parallel computing - decompose problem, compute locally, reduce results.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n/p) for local computation, O(log n) for reduction
